commentary: Ten activities this week versus thirteen last week, so yes, the volume dropped, but honestly the quality tells a more interesting story. That Saturday half marathon equivalent came in at 159 bpm average against last week's 151, which means you pushed harder on the same distance — the legs were working, not just turning over. The swim block held beautifully, with three solid Prince Alfred sessions sitting in that 134 to 149 bpm range, and Monday's 2050m at 149 felt genuinely effortful in the best way. What went missing was Wednesday's training entirely, and the weights sessions this week ran a little quieter in heart rate terms, which suggests either smart recovery or a mid-week dip in energy you probably already know about. The morning walk on Friday was a very cute addition — very "I am taking care of myself and I look good doing it" energy, and that counts.
metrics_commentary: Okay that swim volume is genuinely obscene in the best possible way — 7414 metres means you were basically just living in the water this week, which tracks because you clearly have the shoulders for it. The run sessions are lean on frequency but 15.9 km still got done, and honestly two solid outings beats five mediocre ones every time. Your HRV is the one place where I'm giving you a gentle side-eye, because 55 sitting below your 7-day average of 58 tells me your body is carrying something — stress, sleep, a big week — and it's worth actually resting rather than just performing rest. VO2 Max at 56.1 is genuinely hot, full stop, and I will be bringing it up unprompted at parties.
gym_commentary: Four for four, baby — full week, no excuses, no skipped sessions, just Matt showing up every single time Scott asked him to. And that bench press number is not a small thing: 31.7 kg estimated one-rep max up from 28.5 kg is a real jump, the kind that means something is clicking in the chest and shoulders and the whole system that holds a person together. Volume came in lighter this week at 19,484 kg versus last week's 22,062 kg, but that's not a red flag — that's a body being trained smart, not just hammered, and those top-end strength numbers across quads, hinge, back, and chest are sitting exactly where a man who is quietly getting stronger should have them. The becoming is very much on schedule.
